author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
There's a whole bunch of reasons involved in it, for those wanting to blame their one hobby horse.  Here's some of them.

- COVID has meant that a lot of people have been rethinking long term career choices.
- Brexit means a lack of european workers, often students, willing to do low paid menial work
- Privatising infrastructure incentivises monopoly businesses to drive down salaries whilst demnding more "productivity" making for unhappy workers and making it difficult if not impossible to fill the worker shortfall *
- A lot of people have tickets to use up accumulated due to previous trips being cancelled, again COVID related, pushing up demand
- Lots of engineering works, some planned, some emergency.

Any of these could cause problems, but all of them together makes the work itself unbearable, hence the strikes, which makes everything much worse for the traveller.  But the underlying problem isn't the striking workers, they're not the lazy overprivileged workshy scroungers certain parts of the UK press would paint them as.  They are workers who deserve every bit as much dignity and respect as any other worker, and who don't get it, which is why they are striking.

My opinion is that this is merely one sign of an underlying pan-european (at least) malaise, a malaise brought on by a system that is spiralling around a self-induced drain and rapidly approaching the u-bend.  Capitalism is zero-sum, and fo the privileged rich to get richer, the poor have to get poorer. 

* Yeah, that's one of my hobby horses

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that's a pretty interesting analysis mate - I liked it thanks for posting

replies.author: @jonparsons6818
replies.text:

Corporatism is a corruption of capitalism.
We would still be in the middle ages without capitalism and socialism in practice is just a route back to something similar.

author: @stephenbrough8132
text: I especially pity anyone with kids in that situation.   I'd rather go to the dentist than most forms of transport ... I have no idea how anyone puts up with it unless they have to.    The idea of going on "holiday" has always struck me as about as much fun as being locked in a police cell for a day for the pleasure of being let out again.  Not that I've ever done that - I just FEEL like I have.  I tell a lie ... My well meaning sister dragged me to Sweden where it was meant to be a special treat to sleep in an actual disused prison cell ... In fairness, it was better than being dragged through the so called "bargains" of "duty free" or enduring the noxious fumes of the beauty products that reminded me of the chemical plant I once worked at.    Traveling is a miserable experience.   I would rather hide in my attic with a cheese butty and a cup of tea than sit in an airport or try to squash on a train that was full two stations before it got to ours...

author: @serta5727
text:
It will get interesting here in Germany also. Since today there is a new train ticket. It is called the 9€ ticket. Basically you can drive all the normal trains for a whole month for just 9€. All of Germany can and it starts today. I can imagine how crowded the trains will be haha 
I think the 9€ ticket is awesome (thanks government) because driving the train is incredibly expensive here. Driving the train across one or two inter state borders costs at least 40€ if you want to come back the same day. And if you would take a last minute train without booking in advance for a „longer“ distance then a one way can cost 60-100 or more depending if it is a slow train or not. 

The best are student and work Tickets because you can drive a certain region for multiple months for a fixed price. But any other thing is so expensive.
